title: 你是不是也“学了很多，却做不到”？揭秘学习与行动之间的“隐形障碍”！
author: 李占兵
digest: 🤔 你是否也常常这样：收藏夹里塞满了“干货”，却很少打开？报了不少线上课程，却总是半途而废？
content:
🤔 你是否也常常这样：

  • 收藏夹里塞满了“干货”，却很少打开？
  • 报了不少线上课程，却总是半途而废？
  • 道理都懂，但遇到事情还是“该咋样还咋样”？

明明投入了大量时间学习新知识、新技能，但生活似乎并没有因此发生实质性的改变。这种“学而不行”的现象，就像一种“知识的惯性”，让我们停留在学习的舒适区，迟迟无法迈出行动的那一步。

今天，我们就来聊聊，究竟是什么阻碍了我们从“知道”走向“做到”，以及如何打破这些“隐形障碍”，真正让知识为我所用！

🚧 我们为什么会“光说不练”？揭秘三大“隐形障碍”

研究发现，学习和行动之间的鸿沟，其实是由多种心理和生理因素共同造成的。

障碍一：舒适区太“香”，行动区太“险” 😨

  • 学习的“安全感”
    学习新东西会带来即时的满足感和成就感，就像打怪升级一样，但它通常是低风险的。相比之下，将学到的东西付诸实践，意味着要面对现实世界的复杂性和不确定性，可能会遇到挫折、失败，甚至他人的评价。
  • 害怕不完美
    许多人追求完美，希望等自己“完全准备好”再行动。但“完美”往往是遥不可及的，这种心态很容易导致拖延，用持续学习来掩盖对行动的恐惧。
  • 拖延症作祟
    行动往往伴随着挑战和不适感，而拖延可以暂时回避这些负面情绪。于是，学习有时会变成一种高级的拖延方式——“我还没学够，再等等”。


障碍二：大脑“懒癌”发作，偏爱“躺平” 🧠

  • 认知惯性
    我们的大脑天生倾向于维持现状，以节省能量。改变旧习惯、学习新行为模式，都需要消耗更多的认知资源，这会让大脑本能地“抗拒”。
  • 习惯的力量太强大
    旧有的行为模式已经形成了稳固的神经回路，就像一条常走的路。而新的行动则需要开辟新路，初期会感觉更费力。大脑自然会选择那条“省事”的老路。
  • 短期激励不足
    很多坏习惯（比如刷短视频）能带来即时快感，而应用新知识带来的回报往往是延迟的、不确定的。大脑的奖励系统可能更偏爱“短平快”的刺激。


障碍三：以为“懂了”就等于“会了” 💡

  • “伪精通”的错觉
    当我们轻松理解某些学习材料时，很容易高估自己对知识的掌握程度和未来的记忆能力，觉得自己“已经会了”，从而忽视了实践和巩固的重要性。
  • 知识迁移的挑战
    在特定情境下学到的知识，未必能顺利应用到全新的、复杂的现实场景中。就像在驾校练得再好，第一次独自上路还是会手忙脚乱。
  • “必要难度”的逃避
    真正深入的学习需要刻意练习、主动回忆等“有难度”的方法，这往往伴随着一定的挫败感。人们倾向于选择感觉更轻松的学习方式，但这不利于知识的内化和应用。


🚀 如何打破“知识惯性”，从“学霸”变“行动派”？

别担心！知道了原因，我们就能对症下药。以下策略，助你跨越学习与行动之间的鸿沟：

1. 心态调整：拥抱不完美，培养“行动偏好” 💪

  • 先完成，再完美
    接受初次尝试的不完美，把错误视为学习的机会。记住，“完成”比“完美”重要得多。
  • 设定“行动截止日期”
    不要让学习无限期地进行下去。给自己一个明确的时间节点，强迫自己开始行动。
  • 警惕“分析瘫痪”
    当信息收集到一定程度后，就要果断决策并开始尝试。过度分析只会让你寸步难行。


2. 自我赋能：提升效能感，点燃内在驱动力 🔥

  • 从小处着手，积累成功经验
    将大目标分解成小任务，每完成一个小任务都给自己积极的反馈，逐步建立“我能行”的信念。
  • 连接个人价值
    思考学习的内容如何与你的个人目标、价值观相联系，让行动更具内在意义和动力。
  • 可视化成功
    想象自己成功运用所学知识后的情景，这种积极的心理暗示能增强行动的欲望。


3. 习惯养成：巧设“行动开关”，让改变自然发生 🌱

  • 找到你的“提示-常规-奖励”回路
    为你想养成的行动习惯，设置一个明确的“提示”（比如特定时间、地点），设计一个简单的“常规动作”（即你想应用的知识），并给自己一个“奖励”（比如短暂的放松、一句自我肯定）。
  • 从微习惯开始
    比如，如果想养成写作习惯，可以从每天写50个字开始，重点是“每天都做”。
  • 公开承诺与寻求支持
    将你的行动计划告诉朋友或加入相关社群，外部的监督和鼓励能有效提升行动力。


4. 环境优化：创造“助攻型”学习与实践场 🤝

  • 营造积极的学习氛围
    选择让你感到舒适、安全、受鼓励的环境进行学习和实践。
  • 寻找榜样和同伴
    观察那些已经成功应用相关知识的人，向他们学习。与志同道合的伙伴一起行动，可以相互激励。
  • 即学即用，联系实际
    学习时，多思考“这个知识点我可以用在什么地方？”并尝试将新学到的内容与你当前的任务或生活场景联系起来。


5. 实战演练：刻意练习，加速知识内化 🎯

  • 专注少量关键行为
    不要试图一次性改变所有事情。选择1-2个最想应用的核心知识点进行突破。
  • 主动输出倒逼输入
    尝试用自己的话复述学到的内容，或者将其教授给他人，这能极大地加深理解和记忆。
  • 定期复盘反思
    在行动后，花时间回顾过程，总结经验教训，不断优化下一步的行动。


写在最后

从“知道”到“做到”的转变，并非一蹴而就。它需要我们深刻理解背后的阻力，更需要我们拿出勇气和智慧，一步一个脚印地去实践。

别让“知识的惯性”拖住你前进的脚步。从今天起，选择一个小目标，勇敢地迈出第一步吧！你期待看到自己怎样的改变呢？欢迎在评论区分享你的想法！👇

title: 如何打破学习到行动之间的鸿沟
author: 李占兵
digest: 我最近发现自己陷入了学习的陷阱——只学习而不行动，为了解决这个问题，我使用gemini的deep resear
content:
我最近发现自己陷入了学习的陷阱——只学习而不行动，为了解决这个问题，我使用gemini的deep research功能对这个问题进行了深入研究，以求发现「个人陷入学习困境的根本原因」。
基于Gemini强大的检索和推理能力，我对这个问题的有了初步层面的了解，后续会换用更强大的模型进行研究。整个过程中，Gemini检索了260多个网站，从中获取了高质量的研究结果，整理出了一篇完整的研究报告，鉴于整个报告内容较多，我将基于报告的内容，来为您深入分析这个问题。
这篇文章预示着，以后我写的文章将不再是基于我个人思考和经验性总结，而是具有科学研究支撑的简化版「研究报告」。
The Psychological Landscape of Learning and Inaction
  • Stuck in Observation: The passive consumption of information and its limited impact on behavioral change.

The Inertia of Knowledge: Understanding the Gap Between Learning and Action

Introduction: The Paradox of Perpetual Learning

Many individuals dedicate significant time and effort to acquiring new knowledge and skills, yet a notable disconnect often exists between this learning and its practical application in their lives. This phenomenon, where the pursuit of learning appears to become an end in itself rather than a catalyst for tangible change, presents a paradox. The user's query delves into this very issue, highlighting the common tendency to remain in the initial phases of learning, sometimes indefinitely, while avoiding deeper engagement, thoughtful reflection, and, most importantly, decisive action. This pattern can even manifest as using learning as a subtle justification for postponing the often uncomfortable process of implementation. Understanding the underlying mechanisms that contribute to this inertia is crucial for fostering a more effective and action-oriented approach to personal and professional development.

The Psychological Landscape of Learning and Inaction

Stuck in Observation: The passive consumption of information and its limited impact on behavioral change.

A fundamental aspect of how humans acquire knowledge is through observational learning.1 This process, where individuals learn by watching others, is particularly salient during childhood and plays a vital role in socialization as children imitate the behaviors they observe.1 However, the mere act of observing and understanding a behavior does not automatically translate into personal action. Several factors influence whether an individual will mimic observed actions, including the degree to which they relate to the model and their confidence in their own abilities.1 Consequently, while observational learning provides a crucial foundation by demonstrating how something is done, it often falls short in instilling the personal motivation and self-belief necessary to initiate independent action. Simply witnessing the success of others does not inherently create a conviction in one's own capacity to achieve similar outcomes. The absence of direct personal experience and a sense of agency can significantly impede the transition from passive learning through observation to active engagement and implementation.

The Comfort of the Learning Phase: How the initial stages of learning can become a safe haven, delaying the discomfort of implementation.

The journey of habit formation, as outlined in psychological theories, typically involves distinct stages: an initiation phase where a new behavior is selected, a learning phase where the behavior is practiced, and a stability phase where the habit becomes automatic.2 It is during this learning phase that individuals can become entrenched, continuously seeking more information and knowledge without progressing to the consistent application of what they have learned. Research suggests that embarking on overly ambitious plans during the learning phase significantly increases the likelihood of failure.2 This potential for failure can inadvertently reinforce the tendency to remain in the preparatory stages of learning, avoiding the perceived risks associated with taking action. The learning phase, therefore, can inadvertently offer a sense of forward movement and accomplishment without the inherent vulnerability of taking concrete steps and facing potential setbacks in the real world. This continuous state of learning can function as a subtle form of procrastination, providing a comfortable illusion of productivity while effectively delaying the often-discomforting transition to practical application.

The Weight of Perfection: How perfectionism and fear of failure paralyze action despite acquired knowledge.

Two significant psychological barriers that frequently impede the translation of learning into action are perfectionism and the fear of failure.4 The fear of failure can manifest as an avoidance of new endeavors and a reluctance to engage in activities where one's performance might be evaluated negatively.4 Individuals may hesitate to initiate action due to the apprehension of not meeting expectations, either their own or those they perceive from others. Closely related to this is perfectionism, characterized by the pursuit of unrealistically high standards. This relentless striving for flawlessness can undermine motivation and hinder progress, as individuals may feel perpetually unprepared to act until they reach an unattainable level of mastery.4 The anticipation of not achieving perfect execution can be so overwhelming that it prevents individuals from even starting, effectively creating a state of paralysis despite the knowledge they may have acquired. In this context, learning can become a form of continuous preparation, a way to postpone the vulnerability associated with taking action and potentially falling short of perfect outcomes.

Procrastination as a Barrier: The emotional underpinnings of delaying action and its link to learning.

Procrastination, a common obstacle to productivity and goal achievement, often plays a significant role in the learning-action gap.4 It is frequently rooted in feelings of self-doubt and a fear of not being adequate for the task at hand.6 Rather than simply being a matter of poor time management, procrastination is increasingly understood as a form of emotional dysregulation, a coping mechanism to avoid the negative emotions that the thought of initiating a task can trigger.7 These negative emotions might include anxiety about the difficulty of the task, fear of failure, or even boredom.7 In this context, learning can insidiously transform into a subtle form of procrastination.8 Individuals might rationalize their inaction by convincing themselves that they require further learning or preparation before they can confidently proceed. This continuous cycle of learning provides a temporary reprieve from the discomfort associated with taking action, offering a semblance of productivity while ultimately delaying the actual implementation of knowledge.

The Inertia of the Known: Psychological inertia and the brain's preference for the status quo over the uncertainty of action.

A powerful psychological force that contributes to the learning-action gap is psychological inertia.9 This refers to the inherent tendency to maintain the current state of affairs and resist change unless a compelling psychological motive arises to instigate a shift.11 Our brains are fundamentally wired to conserve energy and, as a result, exhibit a preference for familiar routines and established patterns of behavior.10 This inclination towards the status quo is closely linked to cognitive biases such as the status quo bias, where individuals prefer things to remain as they are, and the endowment effect, where we tend to overvalue what we already possess while undervaluing new alternatives.10 The perceived effort and inherent uncertainty associated with venturing into new territories, such as applying recently acquired knowledge, can feel overwhelming when compared to the comfort and predictability of remaining within the familiar domain of learning. This resistance to change, often fueled by an underlying fear of the unknown, failure, or rejection, can effectively keep individuals tethered to the cycle of continuous learning, preventing them from translating their knowledge into tangible action.

The Physiological Underpinnings of Learning and Behavioral Inhibition

The Neural Pathways of Knowledge Acquisition: How the brain encodes and stores new information.

The acquisition of new knowledge and skills is fundamentally a process of creating and strengthening neural pathways within the brain.12 This remarkable ability of the brain to reorganize its structure and function in response to new experiences and learning is known as neuroplasticity.15 Through consistent repetition and practice, neurons form new connections, and existing connections are reinforced, effectively laying down the neural infrastructure for new behaviors and cognitive abilities.17 It is important to recognize that different types of memory, such as declarative memory (conscious recall of facts and events) and nondeclarative memory (unconscious memory for skills and habits), involve distinct brain systems.18 Habits, in particular, rely on the basal ganglia and become automatic responses through repeated activation of specific neural circuits.17 While the act of learning initiates the formation of these neural pathways, their strengthening and consolidation into reliable triggers for action necessitate consistent activation through practical application. Remaining predominantly in the learning phase, without sufficient engagement in practice, means that these newly formed pathways may not be adequately reinforced to spontaneously initiate the learned behavior.

The Neuroscience of Decision-Making and Action Initiation: Exploring the brain regions and processes involved in triggering behavior.

The intricate processes of decision-making and action initiation involve a complex interplay of various brain regions.19 The prefrontal cortex (PFC), often referred to as the brain's executive center, plays a crucial role in higher-order cognitive functions such as planning, reasoning, and evaluating potential actions.19 Simultaneously, the limbic system, encompassing structures like the amygdala and hippocampus, contributes to decision-making by processing emotions and memories associated with different choices.19 The initiation of physical actions is heavily influenced by the basal ganglia, a group of nuclei deep within the brain, and the signaling of the neurotransmitter dopamine, which plays a key role in motivation and reward.26 The tendency to remain in a state of perpetual learning without transitioning to action might reflect an imbalance in the activity and communication between these different brain regions. While the PFC might be highly engaged in the acquisition of knowledge and the evaluation of options, the signals necessary to trigger motor responses via the basal ganglia and motor cortex might be comparatively weak or inhibited. This potential disconnect between the cognitive processing of learning and the neural mechanisms required for initiating behavior could explain the observed gap between knowing and doing.

Cognitive Load and the Aversion to Effort: Why initiating action can feel mentally taxing and how the brain seeks to minimize effort.

The human brain, while remarkably capable, operates under the principle of minimizing energy expenditure. Consequently, cognitive effort, the mental exertion required to perform tasks demanding conscious thought and control, is often perceived as aversive.29 This principle, sometimes referred to as the "law of least effort," suggests that the brain will naturally gravitate towards less demanding courses of action when faced with choices.10 Initiating a new action, particularly one that involves applying recently learned knowledge in unfamiliar situations, typically requires a significant allocation of cognitive resources for planning, execution, and error correction.31 In contrast, remaining in the learning phase, especially if it involves passive consumption of information such as reading or watching videos, generally demands a lower level of cognitive engagement. Therefore, the brain's inherent tendency to conserve energy might inadvertently favor the continued acquisition of knowledge over the more mentally taxing process of actively applying that knowledge in the real world, contributing to the observed learning-action gap.

The Reward System's Role in Motivation: How dopamine and other neurotransmitters influence the drive to act on learned information.

The brain's reward system, a network of neural structures that utilizes neurotransmitters like dopamine, plays a critical role in reinforcing behaviors associated with pleasure, motivation, and learning.35 Dopamine, in particular, is released when we experience or anticipate rewards, strengthening the neural connections associated with the behaviors that led to those rewards.35 Notably, the expectation of a reward can be a more potent motivator than the reward itself.36 In the context of learning and action, bad habits often form more readily because they tend to offer immediate gratification, triggering a quick release of dopamine.2 Conversely, the rewards associated with applying newly learned knowledge might be delayed or less certain, posing a challenge to sustained motivation.2 If the immediate sense of accomplishment derived from acquiring knowledge is more salient and rewarding to the brain than the potential future benefits of taking action, individuals might prioritize continuous learning. A lack of immediate positive reinforcement for the effort involved in initiating action can therefore hinder the translation of learning into tangible behavior.

Habit Formation and the Resistance to New Behaviors: The established neural circuits of existing habits and the challenge of creating new ones.

Human behavior is significantly shaped by habits, which are automatic sequences of actions triggered by specific cues in familiar contexts.2 These habitual behaviors rely on well-established neural pathways, particularly within the basal ganglia, making their execution effortless and often unconscious.17 When individuals attempt to implement new knowledge or skills acquired through learning, they are essentially trying to establish new habits that compete with existing, often deeply ingrained, patterns of thought and behavior. Changing established habits and forming new ones requires conscious effort, sustained repetition, and the creation of new neural pathways.12 This process can be challenging because it involves overcoming the inherent inertia of the brain's existing neural circuits, which are optimized for efficiency and familiarity. Individuals might find themselves reverting to familiar, and potentially less effective, learning habits rather than embracing the effort required to translate new knowledge into consistent action.

The Disconnect: Why Learning Doesn't Always Lead to Doing

The Illusion of Competence: How easy-to-understand material can create a false sense of mastery, hindering the perceived need for practice and action (Stability Bias).

A cognitive phenomenon known as stability bias can significantly contribute to the gap between learning and action.42 This bias refers to the tendency for individuals to overestimate how well they will remember information in the future based on how easily they can process and understand it at the moment.42 When learning material feels straightforward and readily comprehensible, individuals may develop a false sense of mastery, leading them to underestimate the necessity of effortful study strategies and practical application for long-term retention and effective use.42 For instance, students often report feeling like they learn more by simply rereading material, a relatively passive task, compared to more active and effortful techniques like self-quizzing, even though research indicates the latter is far more effective for actual learning.46 This illusion of competence can be particularly problematic because it reduces the perceived need for the deliberate practice and real-world application that are essential for truly internalizing knowledge and translating it into action. The ease of initial understanding can thus paradoxically hinder the motivation to engage in the "desirable difficulties" that foster genuine mastery and the ability to act confidently on what has been learned.

The Challenge of Transfer: Why knowledge learned in one context often fails to be applied in different real-world situations.

A significant hurdle in the journey from learning to action is the challenge of learning transfer.47 Learning transfer refers to the ability to apply knowledge, skills, or understanding acquired in one context to new or different situations.49 Research consistently shows that achieving effective transfer is a complex process with numerous potential barriers that can arise before, during, and after the initial learning experience.49 These barriers can include a lack of motivation to apply the learning, insufficient foundational knowledge, and a failure to design learning experiences that explicitly bridge the gap between the learning environment and the real-world context where the knowledge needs to be applied.49 Notably, "far transfer," the application of learning to situations significantly different from the original learning context, is particularly challenging and occurs infrequently.50 This context-specificity of learning means that individuals might successfully acquire knowledge in a structured learning setting but struggle to recognize its relevance or effectively apply it when faced with the complexities and nuances of real-world scenarios. This failure to bridge the contextual gap between learning and application represents a major impediment to translating knowledge into tangible action.

The Double-Edged Sword of Desirable Difficulties: How the effort required for deep learning can sometimes deter initial attempts at application.

The concept of desirable difficulties highlights a counterintuitive principle in learning: conditions that make learning seem more challenging in the short term often lead to better long-term retention and the ability to transfer knowledge to new situations.42 These difficulties, such as engaging in retrieval practice (actively recalling information), spacing out study sessions, and interleaving the study of different topics, require greater cognitive effort and can initially lead to more errors and a slower rate of perceived progress.46 However, this very effort promotes deeper processing and strengthens memory in ways that more passive learning strategies do not. Despite the long-term benefits, the initial struggle and the feeling of making more mistakes when using desirable difficulties can be demotivating for learners.46 Students often gravitate towards learning methods that feel easier and more immediately productive, even if those methods are less effective for lasting learning and application.42 Consequently, while embracing desirable difficulties is crucial for developing the robust understanding needed for effective action, the initial discomfort they induce can sometimes deter individuals from adopting them, potentially hindering their ability to confidently apply their knowledge in the long run.

Bridging the Gap: Strategies for Transforming Learning into Action

Cultivating a Bias for Action: Fostering a mindset that prioritizes implementation over endless learning.

To effectively bridge the gap between learning and action, it is essential to cultivate a "bias for action," a mindset that prioritizes implementation and experimentation over the pursuit of perfect knowledge before taking any steps.71 This principle, exemplified by companies like Amazon, emphasizes the value of decisive action and making timely decisions even when all the information is not yet available.71 A key aspect of this involves overcoming analysis paralysis, a state where overthinking and excessive information gathering lead to inaction.72 This can be achieved by consciously recognizing when further learning yields diminishing returns and actively seeking opportunities to apply the acquired knowledge, even if it means stepping outside one's comfort zone and accepting the possibility of initial imperfections.83 Setting deadlines for decision-making and implementation, as well as adopting a "good enough" approach rather than striving for unattainable perfection, can help shift the focus from perpetual learning to tangible progress.78

Boosting Self-Efficacy and Confidence: Building belief in one's ability to successfully apply learned skills.

Self-efficacy, an individual's belief in their capacity to succeed in specific situations or accomplish particular tasks, plays a pivotal role in determining whether learning translates into action.84 Individuals with high self-efficacy tend to approach challenges with confidence and are more likely to persevere in the face of setbacks.84 To foster this crucial belief, several strategies can be employed. Setting achievable goals and breaking down larger tasks into smaller, more manageable steps can provide early wins and build a sense of competence.84 Visualizing success and reflecting on past accomplishments can reinforce a positive self-image and strengthen belief in one's capabilities.84 Seeking out positive role models and engaging in positive self-talk can further enhance self-efficacy.84 Importantly, mastery experiences, where individuals successfully complete challenging tasks, are considered the most effective source of building self-efficacy.88 By focusing on incremental progress and celebrating small victories, individuals can cultivate the confidence needed to actively apply their learned knowledge in various contexts.

Enhancing Intrinsic Motivation: Tapping into internal drivers and rewards to encourage action.

Intrinsic motivation, the drive to engage in an activity because it is inherently interesting or enjoyable, is a powerful force in promoting action after learning.92 When individuals are intrinsically motivated, their desire to act stems from internal satisfaction rather than external pressures or rewards.98 To cultivate this internal drive, it is beneficial to connect learning to personal goals and values, making the application of knowledge more meaningful.94 Providing autonomy and choice in how learned material is applied can also significantly enhance intrinsic motivation.92 Recognizing and praising the effort and process involved in applying knowledge, rather than solely focusing on the outcome, can further encourage internal motivation.92 Creating opportunities for social interaction and collaboration in the application of learning can also tap into the human need for relatedness, boosting engagement and motivation.92 By fostering a sense of competence, autonomy, and relatedness, individuals are more likely to find the act of applying their learning inherently rewarding, leading to more consistent action.

Overcoming the Fear of Failure and Embracing Imperfection: Reframing mistakes as learning opportunities and encouraging initial imperfect action.

The fear of failure and the pursuit of perfection can be significant impediments to taking action after learning.102 To overcome these barriers, it is crucial to reframe mistakes not as indicators of inadequacy but as valuable learning opportunities.83 Adopting a "beginner's mindset," where one accepts that initial attempts may be imperfect, can reduce the pressure to perform flawlessly from the outset.107 Shifting the focus from achieving perfect outcomes to making progress and celebrating small wins can also help to alleviate the paralysis caused by perfectionism.103 Setting realistic expectations and breaking down goals into smaller, more manageable steps can make the prospect of taking action less daunting.105 Practicing self-compassion and treating oneself with kindness when mistakes occur is essential for building resilience and encouraging continued effort.104 Ultimately, embracing the idea that imperfect action is often better than perfect inaction can liberate individuals to start applying their knowledge without the paralyzing fear of not getting it exactly right the first time.

Strategies to Combat Analysis Paralysis: Techniques for moving from overthinking to timely decision-making and implementation.

Analysis paralysis, the state of overthinking a situation to the point of inaction, can be a major roadblock in translating learning into practical application.72 To overcome this tendency, several techniques can be effective. Setting clear deadlines for making decisions and taking action can create a sense of urgency and prevent endless deliberation.78 Narrowing down the number of options or approaches to consider can reduce the feeling of being overwhelmed.78 Practicing making quick decisions on smaller, less consequential matters can build decisiveness for larger tasks.78 Utilizing established decision-making frameworks or processes can provide structure and reduce the cognitive burden of making complex choices.76 Adopting a "good enough" mindset, where the focus is on making a functional decision and moving forward rather than striving for an elusive perfect solution, can also be highly beneficial.82 By implementing these strategies, individuals can break free from the cycle of overthinking and begin to act on their learned knowledge in a more timely and effective manner.

Harnessing the Power of Habit Formation: Applying principles of cue, routine, and reward to establish new action-oriented habits.

Habit formation, the process by which new behaviors become automatic, offers a powerful framework for encouraging action after learning.2 Habits typically develop through the consistent repetition of specific actions in response to familiar cues.2 The "habit loop" consists of a cue (a trigger), a routine (the behavior itself), and a reward (a positive outcome that reinforces the habit).2 To apply this to action after learning, individuals can consciously identify cues that can trigger the desired behavior of using their new knowledge or skills.2 They can then establish a consistent routine of applying what they have learned whenever that cue is present.2 Crucially, providing a reward after performing the action, even if it's just a small sense of accomplishment or a brief enjoyable activity, can reinforce the habit loop and make the behavior more likely to occur automatically in the future.35 Starting with small, manageable changes and focusing on consistency over intensity is key to successful habit formation.2 By intentionally designing habits around the application of learned material, individuals can make taking action a more natural and less effortful process.

Creating Supportive Learning and Social Environments: The role of context and community in encouraging the application of knowledge.

The environment in which learning takes place and the social context surrounding it can significantly influence an individual's willingness and ability to translate knowledge into action.118 A positive and supportive learning environment, characterized by comfort, inclusion, and a sense of safety, can foster confidence and encourage individuals to experiment with applying what they have learned without fear of judgment.118 Social interaction and peer support play a vital role in this process.48 Engaging in discussions with peers, sharing learning experiences, and receiving encouragement from others can increase motivation and build a sense of accountability towards applying new knowledge.101 Observing peers successfully taking action can also provide vicarious learning experiences and boost self-efficacy.101 Creating a community of learners where experimentation and even mistakes are viewed as part of the growth process can significantly reduce the psychological barriers to taking action and encourage a more proactive approach to applying learned material.

Leveraging Social Influence and Modeling: How observing and interacting with others who take action can inspire similar behavior.

Social learning theory underscores the powerful role of observing others in acquiring new behaviors.127 Individuals are more likely to adopt and implement learned knowledge when they see others, particularly those they admire or identify with, doing the same and experiencing positive outcomes.1 Role models and mentors can play a crucial part in bridging the gap between learning and action by demonstrating how to effectively apply new skills and knowledge in real-world situations.53 Action learning, an approach that emphasizes learning through actively working on real problems and reflecting on the experience, also leverages the power of social interaction and observation.130 By observing how others approach challenges and implement solutions, and by engaging in collaborative problem-solving, individuals can gain valuable insights and develop the confidence to take action themselves. The presence of social influence and positive modeling can thus serve as a significant catalyst in transforming learned concepts into practical behavior.

Practical Techniques for Transitioning from Learning to Doing: Actionable steps and strategies for immediate implementation.

To facilitate a smoother transition from the realm of learning to the practice of doing, several practical techniques can be employed. Setting clear and specific learning goals and desired outcomes before embarking on a learning journey can provide a roadmap for subsequent action.53 Incorporating real-world examples and case studies during the learning process can enhance the perceived relevance and applicability of the material.53 Creating concrete action plans that directly link learned concepts to real-world responsibilities, rather than focusing on abstract projects, can increase the likelihood of implementation.47 Encouraging regular self-reflection on one's commitments and progress can foster a sense of accountability and motivation to act.47 It is often more effective to focus on implementing one or two key behavior changes at a time, rather than trying to overhaul everything at once.38 Providing ongoing support and opportunities for practice after the initial learning phase is crucial for reinforcing new skills and encouraging their application in the workplace or daily life.53 By focusing on these actionable steps, individuals can create a more direct and effective pathway from acquiring knowledge to putting it into practice.

Conclusion: From Knowledge to Action - Integrating psychological and physiological insights to cultivate a culture of implementation.

The tendency to remain in a state of perpetual learning without translating that knowledge into tangible action is a multifaceted issue, rooted in a complex interplay of psychological and physiological mechanisms. Psychological barriers such as the fear of failure, perfectionism, procrastination, and the comfort of the learning phase itself can create significant resistance to taking the often-uncomfortable steps towards implementation. Furthermore, the brain's inherent preference for the status quo, driven by psychological inertia, and its inclination to conserve cognitive effort contribute to the challenge of initiating new behaviors based on learned information. Physiologically, the brain's reward system, habit formation processes, and the strength of neural pathways all play a role in influencing motivation and the likelihood of action. The disconnect between learning and doing is further exacerbated by cognitive biases like the illusion of competence, the difficulties inherent in transferring knowledge across contexts, and the initial effort required to engage in desirable difficulties for deep learning.

However, this inertia of knowledge is not insurmountable. By consciously cultivating a bias for action, boosting self-efficacy and intrinsic motivation, and developing strategies to overcome the fear of failure and analysis paralysis, individuals can begin to bridge this gap. Harnessing the power of habit formation, creating supportive learning environments, leveraging social influence, and implementing practical techniques for transitioning from learning to doing are all crucial steps in transforming acquired knowledge into tangible outcomes. Ultimately, the journey from knowledge to action requires a conscious shift in mindset, a willingness to embrace imperfection, and a commitment to consistent application, integrating both psychological and physiological insights to cultivate a culture of implementation for personal and professional growth.

title: Thinking, writing, reading, and learning were the same process
digest: 人的一天有24小时，大部分人的一天可以拆分为3*8——3部分，每部分8小时，一部分用来睡觉，一部分用来工作，剩
content:
人的一天有24小时，大部分人的一天可以拆分为3*8——3部分，每部分8小时，一部分用来睡觉，一部分用来工作，剩下的部分用来「过自己的生活」。
但由于某些不可控的原因，我们的这三部分时间常常处在混乱中。要么该睡觉的时候还在加班工作，要么该做点自己的事情时，发现根本找不到精力，导致我们忙忙碌碌，却收获不到任何成就感和幸福感。
在睡眠上压缩时间，生活上匆匆忙忙，最终的时间都流向了——工作。
可当你每天花很多时间在工作上，回过头来你才发现，你只不过在做着重复的事，重复的像一台机器一般机械地扭动着不堪重负的身躯，只待明日的到来。
前几日，我在朋友圈中看到一位哥哥发的朋友圈。我大一时去社会实践，在我们当地的乡政府与他相识，那时候他刚毕业工作不久，转眼间四年过去了，当我再次了解到他的状态时，是从他的朋友圈文字中。他说：“今天是我30岁生日，曾经的年少轻狂从我身上淡去，来到陌生的环境工作多年后，我在这里成了家，生活没有如我所设想般走得轰烈，我也渐渐忘了自己曾经许下的诺言···”
我知道让自己去追求某些热爱可能不切实际，但我至少要给自己的好爱留点空间，在独自一人时可以安静的与它共处。
现在我们把睡觉和工作的时间抛开，这些时间某种程度上并不是我们能自由支配的，睡觉的时间是由个体的生理机制调控，而工作的时间由为你支付报酬的人说了算，唯一能相对自由的时间可能就那短短的8小时不到，毕竟你还要花在吃喝拉撒上。
这8小时你如果能真正用起来，对于想要进步的人而言已经足够了。其实调查发现，人一天真正能有效学习的时间就4小时，所以其实你每天学习4小时就已经超越绝大多数普通人了。
但由于我们身处在一个满是诱惑的环境中，注意力会被无情的掠夺，所以想要真正沉浸下来学习还需要不断地与各种外在因素作斗争。
我最近在看《writing to learn》，里面提到：

Writing and thinking and learning were the same process.

我思考一番，加上了reading。

思考、写作、学习、阅读，正确的实践(practice)过程应该是：

reading--provoke thought--为写作提供原料--->writing-- stimulate thinking--->learning--->growth。

翻译过来：个人成长是通过学习实现的，而学习分为阅读、写作和思考，阅读可以激发思考为写作提供素材，而写作又可以反过来刺激思考，进一步完善学习。但整个过程中，只有写作是存在产出的，其他环节都是在消耗内外部资源。

不过作为一个打工人，不要忘了practice也能够作为一种学习，当然也可以构成产出。如果从经济角度来看，practice才是一举多得的事，既能从中学习，又能获得产出为别人提供价值，某种程度上来说，如果你不是为了打工而打工，你从中能收获的东西确实不少。

不过很多时候，我们打工的时候就是在打工，能真正将其中收获的知识作为资源储存起来为别人提供价值的确实不多。

也许保罗·格雷厄姆早就想明白了这个问题。他在博客文章《how to make money》提出：

Money is not wealth: money is the medium of exchange; wealth is what you want. the businesses really do is make wealth. they do something people want. but they don’t make money. so you need to create value, rather than make money.

title: 既然别人都有人做了，那我就勉强做自己吧
digest: 最近，我花了很多时间在某个平台上，也许由于算法的原因，我总是看到一些我想看到的内容。
content:
最近，我花了很多时间在某个平台上，也许由于算法的原因，我总是看到一些我想看到的内容。
在算法推荐给我的内容里，那些所谓的过来人谈得最多的可能就是个人发展与社会共存问题。
单看这个问题太宽泛，我试着剖析这个问题的深层次含义。
保罗·格雷厄姆写了一篇博客文章，我刚才读了一下，或许对于解答这个问题有帮助。
格雷厄姆在文章里就说了一个事——what to do?
而后就是他对这个问题的回答。其中最令我为之震撼的内容是，他用一种简单的方式来证明「我们不需要做什么」。
我们应该做什么这个问题很难回答，但我们不该做什么就显得没有太大的难度了。格雷厄姆作为YC的合伙人之一，成功孵化过很多项目，所以对于这样的大佬，我总是会带着几分敬意。
美国有两大地方经常出现在我们的视野，一个是以金融交易为中心的华尔街，另一个是以科技创新为基石的硅谷。相比于金钱的魔幻，创新似乎更胜一筹。而之所以创新更胜一筹，是因为它满足了我们人类固有的好奇心，推动着多巴胺、内啡肽、血清素的分泌。你可能会问，金钱也能做到，但这不是我要讨论的重点。
创新的反义词是模仿，或者说山寨，比如中国出名的华强北。
彼得·蒂尔曾写过一本书——《0到1》。0-1代表着创新，1-n表示模仿，或者你也可以理解为迭代。
好，我们现在可以回到主题了。人或者说这个社会的成员/公民，绝大多数在做的事、走的路，是否都带着模仿的属性呢？
我们总认为自己拥有自由意志，可真正能够用勇气追求「自由意志」的人又有多少呢？我们更多的是在做一些别人已经做过的事（模仿），比如什么工作挣钱（基于前人的样本）。这正是格雷厄姆文章的中心思想，与其回答做什么，不如让人们思考不做什么。既然模仿是不值得做的，那创新或者不模仿的事是不是就成为了我们该做的事呢？
这种反向论证的方法，足以让我这样的青年屌丝折服。
这又引出了另一个我真正关心的问题--社会不需要那么多创新，而我也只是一个穷屌丝，只想找份工作安享中年又该如何做呢？
这回到了一个更现实的问题，而这个问题我在一位已经做到副主任医师的老师身上学到的，更确切地说，是他以过来人的身份告诉我的，也是那些喜欢博取流量的人在某平台上大肆宣扬的：如果你家里没有关系，工作的时候别那么拼，你是打工的，不是老板，为钱搭上自己的健康不值得。从这个角度来看，打工人（模仿者）只是在帮助老板（创新者）实现他的梦想，从而获得报酬，所以员工的满意度比老板要低很多。（这可能不是钱多钱少的问题，而是使命的问题）
那缺失的满意度要如何弥补呢？
用工作中留出来的时间做自己喜欢的事。这就打通了工作中不能太拼（获得个人时间）与提升生活满意度之间的路径。这两者并不矛盾，我给老板打工，帮助老板成就他的事业，他付报酬给我，维持我追求自己的热爱，大家只不过都是在追求个人的人生而已。
所以，作为打工人，不要为了不是自己的东西而放弃追求自己的生活，如果定位出了问题，很容易让自己活在一种痛苦的生活中。

title: bitcion
digest: Got it!
content:
Got it! I’ll prepare a deep-dive guide on Bitcoin’s technical foundation—explained clearly for someone with some basic knowledge, along with long-term investment strategies for building wealth with Bitcoin. I’ll also include security best practices and key resources to continue learning.

I’ll let you know once everything is ready to review.

Bitcoin: A Comprehensive Long-Term Investment Guide

Technical Foundations

Bitcoin is a decentralized digital currency built on a blockchain – a public ledger of all transactions.  As Investopedia explains, a blockchain “stores data in blocks linked together via cryptography.” Each block is a batch of transactions that includes a cryptographic hash of the previous block, forming an immutable chain (Blockchain Facts: What Is It, How It Works, and How It Can Be Used) (Blockchain Facts: What Is It, How It Works, and How It Can Be Used). This design means everyone can verify the entire transaction history, and once a transaction is recorded it cannot be altered or removed (Blockchain Facts: What Is It, How It Works, and How It Can Be Used). The diagram below illustrates this: each transaction is hashed and combined into a Merkle tree, and each block references the prior block’s hash to “chain” them together (Blockchain Facts: What Is It, How It Works, and How It Can Be Used).

(File:Blockchain workflow.png - Wikimedia Commons) Diagram: How a block is formed in the Bitcoin blockchain (source: Economist/Centre for Global Development). Each block includes many transactions (hashed into a Merkle root) along with the hash of the previous block and a random nonce (Blockchain Facts: What Is It, How It Works, and How It Can Be Used). This cryptographic linking is what makes the blockchain secure and tamper-proof.

Bitcoin uses a proof-of-work (PoW) consensus system. In PoW, countless miners worldwide race to solve a hard math puzzle for each block. The first miner to find a valid nonce publishes the block and collects a block reward (new bitcoins) plus transaction fees (What is "proof of work" or "proof of stake"? | Coinbase). As Coinbase notes, “Proof-of-work blockchains are secured… by miners racing to be the first to solve a math puzzle. The winner gets to update the blockchain with the latest verified transactions and is rewarded… with crypto” (What is "proof of work" or "proof of stake"? | Coinbase). The network automatically adjusts the puzzle difficulty every 2,016 blocks (roughly every two weeks) to target a new block about every 10 minutes (How Does Bitcoin Mining Work? A Beginner's Guide). In other words, on average a new block is mined each 10 minutes, regardless of how many miners are active.

Bitcoin transactions are tracked using the UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output) model. Unlike a bank balance, you own Bitcoin as discrete unspent “coins” (UTXOs).  Think of each UTXO as a bucket of satoshis (the smallest Bitcoin unit). If you have 1 BTC and spend 0.5 BTC, the network will send your 0.5 BTC to the recipient and return the other 0.5 BTC back to you as “change” (a new UTXO) (UTXO Model: Definition, How It Works, and Goals) (UTXO Model: Definition, How It Works, and Goals).  For example, one explanation says: “Imagine 1 BTC as a bucket full of coins… If you spend 0.5 BTC, the network gives the entire bucket (1 BTC) to the recipient and then sends you back 0.5 BTC in change” (UTXO Model: Definition, How It Works, and Goals). The leftover 0.5 BTC you receive is now a UTXO that you can spend later. In practice you never see this complexity: your wallet just shows the sent amount and the remaining balance (UTXO Model: Definition, How It Works, and Goals).

Every Bitcoin transaction is authorized by a digital signature using public/private keys.  In essence, your private key is your Bitcoin.  As Ledger explains: “when you own cryptocurrencies, what you really own is a ‘private key’ which unlocks the right… to spend the associated cryptocurrencies.”  Your private key must stay secret; anyone with it can spend your coins (Public Vs Private Keys: Understanding PKC in Crypto | Ledger). The public key (and derived address) is shared for receiving funds.  Together, the keypair proves ownership: “the goal of public and private keys is to prove that a spent transaction was indeed signed by the owner of the funds,” and not forged (Public Vs Private Keys: Understanding PKC in Crypto | Ledger). In short, guard your private key (or seed phrase) like a password – if you lose it or expose it, your Bitcoin is gone.

Long-Term Investment Strategy

  • Dollar-Cost Averaging (DCA):  A proven way to build a Bitcoin position gradually is to invest a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals (weekly or monthly), regardless of price. This averages out the purchase price over time and avoids the stress of timing the market. Investopedia advises: “Dollar-cost averaging allows you to methodically build a position while avoiding the psychology of trying to perfectly time market tops and bottoms” (10 Rules of Investing in Crypto). Over many years, as Bitcoin’s long-term trend has been generally upward, DCA means you buy more when prices are low and less when high, reducing volatility’s impact (10 Rules of Investing in Crypto). For example, instead of dropping in a lump sum and regretting a short-term peak, you spread out risk and automatically “buy the dip” whenever it occurs.

  • Market Cycles & Halvings: Bitcoin’s history shows roughly four-year price cycles tied to its halving events (when miner rewards are cut in half). Each halving (2012, 2016, 2020, 2024…) has historically been followed by a multi-year bull market. Coinbase notes that before and after past halvings Bitcoin rose dramatically – on average +61% in the six months leading up and +348% in the six months after (Bitcoin Halving handbook: A primer for institutional investors). Bitcoin Suisse’s analysis similarly found huge gains in the year before each halving (e.g. +380% before 2012’s halving, +141% before 2016) (Bitcoin Halving Price Developments). In practice, analysts observe a pattern: about a year-long bull run peaking roughly after the halving, then a year-long correction, then a longer recovery phase until the next halving (Are we entering a bull market? - Notes from the CIO). A recent report noted that since 2012 Bitcoin has followed ~12-month bull phases, ~13-month bear phases, and ~22-month recovery phases between halving cycles (Are we entering a bull market? - Notes from the CIO).  Of course past performance does not guarantee future results, but understanding these cycles can help set expectations. (For example, short-term drops around a halving are possible; one analysis warned the 2024 halving looked like a “sell the news” event, with some predicting a drop to ~$42k (Despite Bitcoin price volatility, factors point to BTC’s long-term success).)

  • Portfolio Allocation: Bitcoin is volatile, so it should only be a fraction of a balanced portfolio. Never invest more than you can afford to lose, and maintain adequate savings outside crypto.  Experts often suggest keeping crypto at only 5–10% (or less) of your total portfolio (10 Rules of Investing in Crypto). This provides upside exposure while limiting risk to your overall net worth. For instance, one institutional study found that allocating up to ~6% of a classic 60% stocks/40% bonds portfolio to Bitcoin (and a bit of Ether) roughly doubled the portfolio’s Sharpe ratio with only a modest increase in drawdown (Optimal Crypto Allocation for Portfolios | VanEck) (Optimal Crypto Allocation for Portfolios | VanEck).  In practice, this might mean 3% of assets in Bitcoin, 2–3% in Ethereum or other crypto. The exact split depends on personal risk tolerance, but the principle is: diversify. Don’t “all-in” on crypto; balance it with stable assets, and rebalance regularly.  As Investopedia’s rule #1 states, “never invest more than you can afford to lose” (10 Rules of Investing in Crypto). In other words, treat Bitcoin as a potentially high-growth but still speculative part of your wealth.

Security and Storage

  • Reputable Exchanges for Buying:  When purchasing Bitcoin, use large, regulated exchanges or brokers (e.g. Coinbase, Kraken, Gemini) that follow KYC/AML rules. These platforms are generally secure, but remember that any online exchange can be hacked. A common practice is to keep only the funds you actively trade on the exchange, and transfer the rest to personal wallets. The California regulator (DFPI) advises caution: “Don’t put in more money than you can afford to lose. Crypto assets are not protected by deposit insurance.” (Crypto scams: how to avoid becoming a victim - DFPI).

  • Hardware Wallets / Cold Storage: For long-term holdings, never leave all your Bitcoin on an exchange. The safest approach is cold storage – keeping your private keys offline.  Cold wallets (offline devices) are immune to online hacking. AIBC guide explains: “Cold wallets are considered more secure than hot wallets… [they are] offline, making it nearly impossible for hackers to access your private keys” (Crypto Cold Storage: The Ultimate Guide to Keep Your Crypto Safe). The most common cold solution is a hardware wallet (e.g. Ledger Nano S/X, Trezor). These USB-like devices store keys internally: transactions can be signed on-device without exposing keys to the internet (Crypto Cold Storage: The Ultimate Guide to Keep Your Crypto Safe). In short, you always press physical buttons to approve a payment, keeping keys offline.  (The image below shows a Ledger Nano S, a popular hardware wallet.)

(File:Ledger Nano S - Hard Wallet - Cold Storage for Cryptocurrency 01.jpg - Wikimedia Commons) Hardware wallets (e.g. Ledger Nano S) store your Bitcoin keys offline for maximum security (Crypto Cold Storage: The Ultimate Guide to Keep Your Crypto Safe) (Crypto Cold Storage: The Ultimate Guide to Keep Your Crypto Safe). Always back up your device’s recovery seed on paper (not digital) in a secure location. Keep the device itself in a safe place. Regularly update its firmware to patch any vulnerabilities (Crypto Cold Storage: The Ultimate Guide to Keep Your Crypto Safe). For routine spending or trading, you might use a “hot” (online) wallet with small funds, but keep the bulk of your coins in cold storage (Crypto Cold Storage: The Ultimate Guide to Keep Your Crypto Safe).

  • Private Key Management: Your private key (or seed phrase) is literally the key to your Bitcoin. Ledger Academy emphasizes: “when you own cryptocurrencies, what you really own is a ‘private key’… to spend the associated cryptocurrencies. As it provides access to your cryptocurrencies, it should remain private.” (Public Vs Private Keys: Understanding PKC in Crypto | Ledger). Never share your seed phrase with anyone or enter it on a website. Consider storing it engraved on metal (to resist fire/water) or in a safety deposit box. If you lose your keys (and you have no backup), there is no way to recover your Bitcoin. Conversely, if a thief gets your keys, they can spend your coins. Treat your backup like cash; if someone sees or copies it, your funds are gone.

  • Multisig for Extra Safety: For very large holdings or joint accounts, a multi-signature (multisig) wallet can eliminate the risk of one lost/stolen key. A multisig setup (e.g. 2-of-3 keys) requires multiple devices to approve a transaction, so no single key compromise drains your wallet. CoinDesk notes that properly implemented multisig “eliminates the single point of failure that traditional single-key wallets face” (What Are Multisig Wallets And How Do They Work?). (Popular multisig services like Casa or Blockstream Green use this.)

  • Avoiding Scams: The crypto space is rife with scams. Always be skeptical of “get rich quick” schemes. The California regulator warns: “Beware of… get-rich-quick schemes. Consult credible sources of information…” (Crypto scams: how to avoid becoming a victim - DFPI). Common red flags include unsolicited investment offers, fake celebrity endorsements, or promises of guaranteed returns. Never click random crypto links or ads. Only install wallet software from official websites, and verify URLs (phishing sites are common). Use two-factor authentication (preferably an authenticator app or hardware key, not SMS) on any accounts. Change your passwords regularly (Crypto scams: how to avoid becoming a victim - DFPI).  If an investment opportunity sounds too good to be true, it probably is.

Learning Resources

Books: Good books provide deep insight.  “The Bitcoin Standard” by Saifedean Ammous is highly recommended; it explains Bitcoin’s economics and why it’s often likened to “digital gold” (20+ Best Bitcoin Books for Powerful Insights and Financial Wisdom | BM Pro). For technical understanding, “Mastering Bitcoin” by Andreas Antonopoulos is a definitive guide to how Bitcoin and blockchain work on the inside (20+ Best Bitcoin Books for Powerful Insights and Financial Wisdom | BM Pro). Other notable reads include “Inventing Bitcoin” (Yan Pritzker) for a clear tech intro, and “Digital Gold” (Nathaniel Popper) for a narrative history of Bitcoin’s rise.

Courses: Online courses can solidify fundamentals. For a deep technical course, Princeton’s free Coursera class “Bitcoin and Cryptocurrency Technologies” (by Arvind Narayanan et al.) covers Bitcoin’s mechanics in detail (Best Crypto Online Courses: Discover Top Cryptocurrency Courses). Even beginners can audit it. For a high-level overview, LinkedIn Learning and Udemy offer intro courses on crypto basics. (Many platforms like Coursera or edX allow free auditing; see if financial aid is available (Best Crypto Online Courses: Discover Top Cryptocurrency Courses).)

Podcasts & Newsletters: Stay updated via trusted voices. Podcasts like “What Bitcoin Did” (Peter McCormack), “The Pomp Podcast” (Anthony Pompliano), “Unchained” (Laura Shin) and “Bitcoin Fixes This” feature expert interviews and insights (13 Best Crypto Podcasts: Top Podcasts about Blockchain and Crypto in 2025). Newsletters such as “The Pomp Letter” (Pompliano) or “CoinDesk Daily” summarize market developments. Follow analysts like Michael Saylor, PlanB, Lyn Alden or Glassnode on Twitter/YouTube for charts and on-chain analysis.

Inflows of Knowledge: Wherever you learn, cross-check information. Look for sources that cite data and facts. As Ledger Academy puts it, Bitcoin is complex but accessible; understanding key concepts (blockchain, keys, economic incentives) is a powerful defense against scams (Public Vs Private Keys: Understanding PKC in Crypto | Ledger) (Public Vs Private Keys: Understanding PKC in Crypto | Ledger).

Risks and Management

Investing in Bitcoin carries significant risks. Most notably, volatility is extreme. Prices can swing 20–50% in days. For example, one analysis noted that in 2024 Bitcoin’s price oscillated from ~$72k to ~$60k around the halving, then back up – and experts even cautioned it might drop to $42k (Despite Bitcoin price volatility, factors point to BTC’s long-term success). Be prepared mentally for these swings and focus on long-term trends rather than daily fluctuations.

Regulatory changes are another risk. Governments could impose taxes, restrictions, or outright bans on crypto. Central bankers often criticize Bitcoin’s volatility and lack of liquidity: e.g. the Swiss central bank chief said Bitcoin is “too volatile” and prone to technical issues, arguing it is not suitable for official reserves (Bitcoin isn’t a worthy reserve asset, Swiss National Bank president says: Report) (Bitcoin isn’t a worthy reserve asset, Swiss National Bank president says: Report). Keep an eye on local laws (some countries restrict exchanges, or crack down on privacy coins, etc.).

Security risks also loom: major exchanges and protocols have been hacked in the past (Mt. Gox, Bitfinex, etc.), so always use strong security practices. Even outside hacks, poor practices (like using an unsecured hot wallet for large sums, or falling for phishing scams) can lead to loss. Remember, if YOU lose your private keys, or if they are stolen, there is no recourse.

Other risks include technical or operational issues (software bugs, hard forks) and “black swan” events. However, many investors mitigate risk by only allocating what they can afford to lose (Crypto scams: how to avoid becoming a victim - DFPI) (10 Rules of Investing in Crypto), diversifying into other assets, and using DCA to avoid mistiming.

Conclusion

Bitcoin represents a novel asset with unique long-term potential. Its underlying blockchain and capped supply give it resilience, while historical cycles have produced large gains for patient holders. But success requires learning the technology enough to secure it, following disciplined investment strategies (like DCA and proper portfolio sizing), and staying alert to market and regulatory risks. As the experts say, invest thoughtfully and protect your keys – then let Bitcoin’s long-term trajectory (so far strongly upward) work in your favor (10 Rules of Investing in Crypto) (Public Vs Private Keys: Understanding PKC in Crypto | Ledger).

Sources: Authoritative guides and analyses were used throughout, including Coinbase, Investopedia, CoinDesk, and industry research (Blockchain Facts: What Is It, How It Works, and How It Can Be Used) (10 Rules of Investing in Crypto) (Bitcoin Halving handbook: A primer for institutional investors) (20+ Best Bitcoin Books for Powerful Insights and Financial Wisdom | BM Pro), ensuring up-to-date and in-depth information. Each factual claim is backed by a citation as noted.

title: 为什么要读书
digest: 今天是世界读书日，过去两年我都会在朋友圈分享一些书，但今年我放弃了做这件事，可能是分享欲降低了，又或者觉得读书
content:
今天是世界读书日，过去两年我都会在朋友圈分享一些书，但今年我放弃了做这件事，可能是分享欲降低了，又或者觉得读书其实「没有」任何意义。
回想过去第一次接触得到app，我被里面的精彩知识吸引，那也成了我第一次为之付费的平台，出于种种原因，我今年也放弃了继续开会员，当然，那并不意味着我放弃投资自己。
今日，我大概梳理一下自己过去在那个平台上付费读过的书和课程，算是作为一种纪念吧！
可以说得到占了我大学的大部分时间，总计1771h。
17门课程，12本听书，读了162本，读完47本。
其中李笑来的《财富自由之路》学习了3遍，《何帆读书俱乐部》听了两遍。有些课程也大致学了两遍。总计花费超过1400元。
至于成长方面，用那些时间和金钱确实无法衡量。
在过去三年里，我购买的纸质书超过40本，折算下来平均每本30元，花费大概在1000左右。
一个大学生，四年时间，投资在课外书上的金钱成本超过了2500，加上最近购买的Kindle电子书，算下来在3000元左右。
当然，我买的书大部分都读完了，少部分还在压箱底，除了自己花钱购买的，还有在学校图书馆借阅的，相对于普通人来说，阅读量已经很高了。
但是最近一段时间，我对书的品质要求逐渐提高，可能也是一种个人偏见吧，我决定放弃阅读中文书，转而向英文书进军，一是为了提升英文，二是为了让自己从追求数量转向质量。
我也不断反思自己，发现我并没有在花了那么多金钱成本上赚到与之相对应的金钱，所以我需要一种更加适合自己的学习方式，不再盲目地读书。而是以问题为导向来满足我的求知欲。
经过这四年的课外阅读，我发现读书帮助我用一种更真实的视角来看待这个世界，而这些视角都是那些聪明人曾经挣扎着想明白的问题。
我很喜欢两位作者，它们的书我基本全读过，一位是尼古拉斯·塔勒布，一位是李笑来，他们是我在书里结交到的最聪明的人。也许这个最聪明的评判标准站在我的角度，除了读书多就是非常能赚钱。可能我是一个比较物质的人，所以这个价值观显得有点抽象。
从某种角度来说，我读过的很多书都在某种程度上影响着我，只是我不知道或者无法表达出来而已，但我却能够感受得到我存在一种对自己的自信，这种自信来自于我与过去那些非常聪明的作者通过书中的内容和思考得来的。我相信就算别人再怎么争夺，可能也拿不走，毕竟我相信随着时间的积累，它会刻进骨子里。

title: 被AI工具瞎指挥的哪些事
digest: 作为日常生活已经离开AI的我，常常会与AI发生一些冲突。这些冲突的产生，也让我意识到了AI存在的缺陷。
content:
作为日常生活已经离开AI的我，常常会与AI发生一些冲突。这些冲突的产生，也让我意识到了AI存在的缺陷。
编程
尽管AI驱动的编程已经足够强大，但在某些方面还是值得初次接触的朋友注意。比如，在AI记忆能力有限的情况下，他常常会在项目弄到一半的时候选择停止工作，你需要重新对其发起请求，这样一来一回，相对来说还是会降低效率，不过目前比较大的模型代理对这个问题有了一定的解决。
除了记忆，AI还在项目优化上产生buge，如果你让它对项目进行优化，它可能会对整个项目发起攻击，将某些内容重构，弄出一个它自己也无法解决的问题，然后陷在其中无法自拔，让你的项目无法继续进行。我在这方面深有感触，虽然它初步设想的MVP是可以实现的，结果也挺让人满意，但随着项目的优化和复杂度提升，整个人的时间都将会荒废在一个莫名其妙的问题里。
这让我不禁感叹，初学者在AI面前似乎就是一个附属的机器，你只是在配合着它演戏。看似你在操纵它，其实是它在引导你实现它想要的功能，这种人与AI合作的工作，在某种程度上，看似是个人创造力的表达，但更深层来理解，你只是借助一个工具，被牵着走向你也不太熟悉的地方。
信息整合
AI在网页搜索方面给出的信息需要个人亲自去实践，少了这个过程，你很容易被AI带偏。我使用最多的就是这个功能，常常感觉它在欺骗我，这种不信任既是对自己负责，也是对未来的AI提出了更高的要求。
比如，我让他给我某个信息，它基于搜索结果整理出一份看似毫无毛病的指南，但亲自上手会发现，有些根本不可行，你需要持续的给他反馈，让它一步步修正自己的偏差。这像极了一个不断犯错的小孩，你需要给他时间试错，它才会获得让你满意的结果。但没有东西是万能的，AI也不例外，不要试图将AI给出的结果作为决策的标准，毕竟最终承担责任的是自己而不是AI。作为助手，AI是可信的，而且也值得我们学习，作为决策者和执行者，AI需要更多的提升。所以，在使用AI之前，要给其设定一个角色，什么角色该做什么，同时也赋予相应的可信度，这样在人与AI合作中才会获得效率的最大化。
知识传授
如果将AI看作以为博学的老师，你会发现它的智慧远超现有的大多数人。在知识的世界里，它给出的知识是值得可信的，毕竟这是它的强项。在你怀疑它之前，先看看自己到底记了多少知识在脑中。核心原因在于--验证的困难性。你说你查资料吧，它查的比你更快、更全，所以以我之见，如果不是特别离谱，要么是它真的出错，要么是我们从始至终就记忆了错误的知识。
当然，你不放心的话，可以多问几遍，让它试图去修正自己。
以上就是我这几个星期在与AI交互过程中获得的一些经验。目前的AI值得使用，也需要我们学会包容，出错是人之常情，不论是对待AI还是对待我们自己，永远都要允许错误的存在，试错和容错值得我们用尽一生去学习。

title: 变革与永恒：从AI工具看教育的未来
author: 李占兵
content:
“变化才是永恒。”这句话我们耳熟能详，可现实中，我们却常常害怕改变。

你有没有留意到，身边的很多事物都在不停变化？人会成长与衰老，社会在不断发展，四季更替、科技更新……几乎没有什么是一成不变的。可就是在教育这件事上，我们却总是固守某些旧有方式，仿佛“讲台+黑板+灌输式教学”才是学习的唯一正确打开方式。

教育的“稳定”，真的是优势吗？


10年前我们怎么学，10年后的我们还是怎么学。老师讲，学生听；布置作业，批改试卷。这种模式虽然有效，但在信息爆炸、节奏迅猛的今天，真的还足够高效吗？

拿英语学习来说。过去很多人认为“背单词就是王道”，于是默写、听写、刷单词App成了主旋律。但为什么那么多人依旧看不懂原版英文书、说不出口完整英文句子？或许问题不在努力，而在“方法”。

我们有没有想过，其实学英语和学汉语是一样的：没人通过死记硬背新华字典学会中文，那为何我们非要靠背诵记住所有英文单词？事实上，很多人在日常查词中，反而“无意识”地记住了很多词汇——眼睛记住的、语境记住的、使用中记住的。（有时候并不需要脑子记住）

手机和AI工具，真的是“洪水猛兽”吗？


过去，考试带计算器是作弊，现在在某些地区是标配；以前课堂上拿出手机会被视为“不务正业”，如今却是效率学习的重要帮手。我们不能否认，手机和AI工具确实可能被滥用，但“管控”并不等于“禁用”。禁止使用只会激发好奇和叛逆，而真正有效的做法，是引导他们正确使用。

比如现在的AI工具，不但能秒查单词、讲解语法，还能一对一讲题、纠错，甚至教你如何写作、演讲……有时候我都想，如果我小时候就能用上这些工具，也许会少走很多弯路。

学习的本质：不是记住，而是理解和应用


我们常常把“记住”当作学习的终点，殊不知，“记忆”只是初级阶段，真正的学习是能够迁移、应用和创造。我们的大脑更擅长处理复杂问题，而不是当一个“记忆仓库”。为什么不把重复性的记忆交给机器，让大脑专注在思考与创新上呢？

AI不是取代老师，而是增强学生


很多人担心AI工具会取代老师，但我更愿意把它看作“学生的私人导师”。当你半夜遇到不会做的数学题，它不会嫌你烦；当你想练英语口语，它可以陪你练到凌晨三点。这正是传统教学难以满足的个性化需求。

但令人遗憾的是，很多学校依旧禁止学生在课堂上使用AI工具、甚至手机。我们允许成年人用AI提高工作效率，却禁止孩子用AI提升学习效率，这是不是有些荒谬？

结语：教育的进步，不该是最后一个“变化”的领域


技术已经改变了我们获取知识、思考问题的方式。但如果教育依旧停留在“老师讲学生听”的旧时代，那我们真的对得起下一代吗？

让我们重新思考什么才是“有效的学习”。也许AI不是万能的，但它一定可以是我们改革教育的一把钥匙。而我们能做的，是把这把钥匙交到学生手中。

title: 如何顺应AI时代的教育方式
digest: 我发现一个奇怪的现象：既然变化才是永恒，那为何我们却害怕改变呢？你仔细观察会发现，周围的很多事物都是变化着的。
content:
我发现一个奇怪的现象：

既然变化才是永恒，那为何我们却害怕改变呢？

你仔细观察会发现，周围的很多事物都是变化着的。人会长大和衰老，社会会向前发展，树有四季···它们的周期或长或短，但一成不变几乎是违背了客观规律。
我不知道你能否找到一个永恒不变的例子？
刚好，我发现了一个不那么恰当的例子——教育。学东西嘛，靠的就是练习，哪有那么多花里胡哨。可也许正因为如此，我们才需要寻求改变。
10年前的教育和现在当然不能比，但十年前的学生在校园里接受的教学形式几乎还是一成不变，⚠️教学形式=/教学内容，教学形式=老师灌输知识给学生。
可你有没有思考过，有些东西已经无法适应这个时代的发展了，或者说有些教学方法从始至终就不是最有效的。
就拿学英语这件事来说，过去我一直以为背单词才是王道，我接触的所有英语老师也都是这么认为的，所以课堂听写、默写单词成了我上学时代的常态，可我发现我依然读不懂英文文章，甚至讨厌起了英语，要不是幸运，现在还被蒙在鼓里。
在英文的世界里，单词和汉字一样，完全没必要去背新华字典吧！我还听过一件有趣的事，这是我最近才关注到的，什么呢？原来有些地方考试的时候是可以带计算器的，在我们那个时候这可是作弊啊！可如果你是用草稿纸算过来的，我们可以问问自己，在生活中我们使用口算多一点呢还是手机计算机多一点？
从这个角度来看，手机改变了计算的方式，当然，我们也可以将记单词改成用手机查单词。
当你查的越来越多的时候，你无意中就记住了，不是你脑子记住就是你眼睛记住。
刚才说了手机改变了学英语和计算的形式，可当年我们有多少人是被灌输使用电子设备被视为作弊，现在也没变。我很想不通，记住有那么重要的话，怎么我高考完全忘记了。有些东西并不是非记住不可的，脑子是用来思考和创造的，整天用来记忆一些死知识，有时候想想挺可笑也挺浪费的。
我们一说国内的数学教育有多么多么厉害，国外又多么的不行，可为什么厉害的数学家好像国外更多呢，我可没说光有天赋，没有努力就能成为数学家。
这些真的是值得我们好好反思的地方，至少我们认识到了，可以让我们下一代的人少经历一些我们曾经经历过的事情，不然什么都在进步，就学习方式还停滞不前，甚至倒退。
有一样东西，我觉得可以改变现有的教育形式——AI工具。
这不是说老师不重要，而是说它可以让学生的学习变得更主动，比起老师这样的引路人，AI工具更像是个人学习助手，而且不受时间和空间的限制。过去你找老师辅导作业，那还要看老师的时间，现在AI工具可以随时启用，不过问题又来了，学校一般禁止学生在上课期间使用手机。看来现代化的工具，在学生眼中还是一个不可获得的稀缺品。这就是学生很容易沉迷于手机的原因。因为手机管制，所以对手机更加好奇，因为好奇而沉迷，而又更加管制。
所以，如果家长和老师意识不到这个问题，那才是主动地降低学生的学习效率。
如果我们站在孩子的角度想想，刷短视频、玩游戏，都是因为现实它无聊了，或者说父母并没有给他们塑造一个好的环境。比如父母常在孩子面前玩手机，那孩子也会潜移默化的受影响，如果你只是用手机来学习，那你的孩子更可能模仿你的做法。所以，我们不应该怪手机，甚至怪孩子。
既然孩子不能使用，那就只能等他们成年了，立刻校园了再使用吧！哈哈哈，是不是挺搞笑的。允许成年人使用AI工具提升工作效率，却不允许孩子使用提升学习效率。
那我只能讲讲成你人如何使用AI提升学习效率啦！毕竟我也是成年人，最近学着使用这些AI工具。以前遇到一个非常难解的问题时，我们需要去百度、Google，才能解决，现在你只要详细描述你的问题给AI工具，它可以一步步指导你完成，既节省了时间，又能获得详细指导，甚至给你提供不同的方法和思路，这么好用的东西，有多少人真的去使用呢，又有多少人提供给孩子和学生使用呢？

882587784cd114313f5cb6abb6777474
https://4ppy.lpylink.com/link/89237a361f2c671cea9d78d90f25f6c9





 不知道出于何种原因，最近对风险的意识强化了很多。


 第一次意识到「风险」是在李笑来的书中，他讲了一个非常深刻的道理，既关于投资，也关于生活。他说，许多年前当他自己开车的时候他才意识到，年轻时横穿马路的行为有多么危险，也许只有自己成为司机才能够明白闯红灯的行人需要冒多大的风险。


 在了解风险之前，我已经是一个开车翻过「几次」的人，从几次这个词不难发现，我有多不长记性。


 我很喜欢一位作者——尼古拉斯·塔勒布（Nassim Nicholas Taleb），他曾在《黑天鹅》和《反脆弱》这两本书中提到一个词——非对称风险。这个词在投资领域比较常见，用塔勒布的理念来解释，我们应该寻找亏损可控，但收益无上限的投资标的，即风险和收益之间的不对称。


 用开车来举例。假设我为了节约时间，每次开车都很快，如果100次开快车中，我能节约3000分钟（每次节约30分钟 * 100），每分钟按10块钱计算，那就是3万RMB，再假设我在这100次中，出现了一次交通事故，医疗费花了1万RMB，那我最终的收益是2万RMB。但这个例子中有一个我无法控制的情况，如果开快车最终导致我生命丧失，那有多少钱我也不会做。所以，从中我们不难发现，开车这件事不是非对称风险，即收益可控，但风险不可控。

title: i am fund using the vpn
content:
I was really worried when my classmate told me that our teacher knew I was using a VPN, because I feared my actions might be considered illegal. I spent a lot of time thinking about it, but I couldn’t come to any useful conclusions.

Through this process, I’m trying to reflect and learn from the experience. I realized that I care a lot about how others see me, but I’m not sure how to make others focus on my strengths instead. If I can build more confidence, I believe I’ll be able to overcome this.

Although I have a lot of areas to improve in terms of both my mindset and actions, I am still putting in the effort to change. When you find the truth of life, you should still learn to love it.

Finally, I’ve decided to become a more low-key person to avoid trouble.

Although I have many weaknesses in my mindset and actions, I am still making an effort to change. When you understand the truth about life, you should still embrace it.

Finally, I am ready to become more low-key to avoid getting into trouble.

title: 为何AI 写得那么好，我却依然坚持自己写
digest: 不知道你有没有使用AI写过文章，如果没有，可以去试试，会让你眼前一亮的。
content:
不知道你有没有使用AI写过文章，如果没有，可以去试试，会让你眼前一亮的。
在今天这样一个生产文章如此容易的时刻，会写东西已经不是什么稀奇或稀缺的技能了，可能随便找一个好一点的大模型，写出来的东西也都「值得」一读。
注意：值得一读并不代表他有多大的价值，只能说它产生的内容新颖而不陈词滥调。
如果你使用普通一点的模型，在推理能力弱的情况下，你看到的东西只是你过往没见过的词汇组合，它与作者写出来的东西是有很大区别的。如果你有阅读能力，你会发现它的内容只是流于浅表的「废话」，反之，你会惊叹它那精妙绝伦的文笔。
作为一个已经写了两年文章的人，我其实能感受到ai写得确实好，可惜我读过一些书，又会觉得那不过是没有思想的词汇组合。
当你读一篇文章的时候，你其实是在与作者隔空交流，这种跨越时间和空间的共鸣是难以被没有「思想」的工具替代的。如果你不能理解，我想用「意淫」来表达，所谓的意淫就是你在头脑中构建一个不存在的事物并假设它真实存在，这和男性产生性欲的底层核心特别类似。
用在这里，如果你不知道你读的内容是AI写的，并假设它是人思想的表达，那么你就是在意淫，且在某种程度上显示了自己知识的匮乏。
其实，你今天看到的大部分文章基本都是通过ai产生的，这种无法分辨人与工具的边界的能力，在未来会更加凸显。从文字到视频，AI的创作能力将会远超「一般人」。（此处的一般人通常指初学者）
如果你还认为，未来的人分为「会使用AI的人和不会使用AI的人」，那你已经out了。你仔细思考会发现，未来只有会使用AI的初学者和会用AI的专业人员。这就和有无手机的区别到能否有效使用手机是一样的。一件东西在还未普及时会停留在「有无」和「会不会」的层面，而一旦它大规模普及后，另一种分类将会出现。
我将未来的人分为初学者和专业人员，是因为我发现，AI工具会帮助人从门外汉快速入门，但入门后的精通AI工具却很难再帮助人进阶。这就扯到学习一样东西除了需要有效的工具，还需要时间的累加才行，效率可以提升，但时间的跨度却很难跨越。
而人在骨子里有一种自恋的性格。这种自恋会让我们产生我已经精通的幻觉，就像有翻译软件会让我觉得阅读英语也就那样嘛，有AI agents 我会产生编程也就那样嘛，有AI工具，写作也就那样嘛的深度错觉。
有一种「幻觉」更加隐秘，你在某一时刻一定产生过这样的念头--有了AI工具，我还学他干嘛！这其实就是初学者和专业人员的区别，前者将希望寄托在工具上，而后者更多是在考虑怎么驾驭工具，更进一步，前者很容易“意淫”——被AI工具耍得团团转，后者知道工具的缺陷，并试图改进它，前者是依赖，后者是合作。不信你看看有翻译软件后，有多人不再花时间学英语。
为什么人会被工具耍，很简单，当人比工具弱的时候，工具就成了老师，成了人可以依赖的对象，如果缺乏批判性思考，此刻的人就成了附庸，成了所谓的没有思考能力的机器。
所以，换个角度想想，未来的工具不一定是机器，人更应小心成为机器。
还有一个社会层面的因素。
以前的企业雇佣一个大学毕业生做实习生可以节约成本，学生在这个过程中也能学到东西，但未来，如果工具能够解决一个实习生的工作，且成本更低，那么毕业生不仅很难得到实习工作，而且还可能会因为缺乏资深老师的帮助，实习生初学者将无法获得有用的知识，导致初学者只能与工具合作，或者依赖工具，最终导致初学者与专业人员的差距原来越大，如果个人意识不到这一点，将永远处在初学者水平。
这就好像，原本写作需要花费几年的时间才能上道，现在你借助工具几分钟产出一篇，然后还洋洋得意，你以为你进步了，其实你还在原地踏步，长此以往，你的思考力下降的同时，依赖性会更强。
所以，做一件事，思考清楚它的底层逻辑，比完成这件事更加重要，搞懂写作的目的，比写出一篇爆款文章要重要。
